segments.title: Kresta class as an iterative Soviet ASW cruiser step
segments.summary: The Kresta classes are presented as iterative Soviet cruiser designs that shifted from Kynda missile limits toward aviation, air defence, and anti-submarine warfare.
segments.body: The episode ties the Kresta story to the Soviet Navy's post-Cuban Missile Crisis reassessment of surface-force and submarine vulnerabilities. Dr. Clarke treats Kynda, Kresta I, and Kresta II as steps along one development path rather than finished endpoints, with each class adjusting missile control, aviation facilities, anti-aircraft weapons, sonar, and ASW missiles. The segment is useful for understanding why the ships could be read both as cruisers and as large anti-submarine ships.

evidence.note: The opening contrasts halted Kynda construction, abandoned larger Shaddock ships, and the Kresta I and II shift toward anti-aircraft, helicopter, and long-range ASW capability.
The transcript says the Kresta designs show a need for aviation facilities, long-range anti-air firepower, and organic anti-submarine capability in a blue-water fleet.
The discussion describes Kynda and Kresta I as starting points in an iterative design approach rather than final products.
The comparison walks through displacement, range, missile, gun, sonar, and helicopter changes from Kynda to Kresta I and Kresta II.
